title: Why AI adoption is blocked by traditional workplaces - LinkedIn
full_text:
Why AI adoption is blocked by traditional workplaces
This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Christopher Manning
1y
Report this post
The biggest blocker for the adoption of AI currently isn't technical AI developments but rather how to re-engineer traditional workplaces and workflows. That last mile can be hard.
Shaun Johnson
1y
Today we announce that AIX Ventures has led the Series A of Workhelix .
Every enterprise must have an AI transformation war room.
Deploying AI will lead to pricing power. When productivity improves through AI, gross- and operating- margins go up. This means price can decrease, bringing more demand and putting the competition on notice.
This isn't an optional transformation. It’s a race. Every enterprise AI transformation war room needs to be staffed with a team that understands the intricacies of the particular enterprise, the data behind the enterprise, economics, and the ability to lead the enterprise through the transformation. And the team needs to have an AI transformation software suite custom to their enterprise, to maximize transformation velocity.
Enter James Milin , Daniel Rock , Andrew McAfee , Erik Brynjolfsson , and Workhelix team. The Workhelix team deploys engineers trained on the Workhelix task-based approach to understand the tasks being completed, specific to a particular enterprise. The engineers partner with the enterprise’s leaders to assess these tasks, and to map bundles of tasks to GenAI solutions.
The war room team members can then partner with various organizations within the enterprise to implement, monitor solution efficacy, and reassess. An effective war room should have every organization engaged in this process, iterating to the best version of that organization within the context of the bleeding edge Gen AI solutions today.
Today AIX Ventures is leading the Workhelix $15 million Series A, along with Andrew Ng 's AI Fund , Accenture Technology Ventures BV, Reid Hoffman , Jeff Dean , Yann LeCun , Sebastian Thrun , Paul Daugherty , Mira Murati, and Jeff Wilke.
I spoke with Rebecca Szkutak at TechCrunch about the company and its massive opportunity to transform our economy and workforce: https://lnkd.in/gJ2pufUc
Enterprise leaders need to have one transformation metric top-of-mind: what % of our enterprise tasks are completed by machines?
34

AI is now being asked to justify itself by showing improvements over existing processes that have not previously been measured in any systematic way. The gap between "good enough we think" and any well-defined metric is hard to bridge.

💡 Sean Makulowich 🔥
9mo
Edited
Report this post
💰 Startups just revealed what they're spending a massive amount of VC money on... Yup, it's AI.
🔎 Andreessen Horowitz analyzed 200,000 startups’ actual AI spend. Not surveys, not hype. CA$H MONEY 💸
💥 60% of AI spend goes to horizontal apps like OpenAI , Anthropic , Canva , Notion , Freepik . And vertical apps still command 40%, with many shifting from augmentation to full workflow automation.
And the signal is LOUD:
🔊 Creative AI tools (image, video, avatars, voice) are among the top 5 spend categories.
🔊 Replit ranked #3 overall, proof that “vibe coding” is no longer a meme. AI is literally building apps that ship.
🔊 In verticals, 12 of 17 top vertical AI apps are augmenting humans, while 5 are already functioning as full “AI employees” (e.g., AI IT desks, AI legal tech, GTM agents, etc.).
💡 Translation for VCs:
➡️ You're not always funding headcount growth; Founders are channeling huge chunks of capital into AI apps.
➡️ Infrastructure raises steal the headlines... but real buy-in is unfolding at the application layer.
➡️ This isn’t experimentation. Startup budgets already treat AI tools as essential, not optional.
➡️ This isn’t an adoption curve. It’s adoption baked into burn rates.
➡️ The next AI unicorns won’t necessarily be infra monsters; they’ll be the apps quietly swallowing VC dollars, one Amex swipe at a time.
👉 Full report: https://lnkd.in/dMEZ-rdE

Neil Bhattacharya
9mo
Edited
Report this post
🚀 Where Startups are spending their dollars💵
Not just another report on AI tools. This is interesting - the guys at a16z just released a AI Spending Report that looks at where 200,000+ startups are actually spending their money on AI tools.
💡Why this matters?
We know that startups are early indicators of adoption trends because they experiment faster so the pattern can be like this 👉 Start-ups adopt → prove ROI → enterprises follow couple of years later.
🚀 Startups are ideal testers because:
• They're not locked into legacy systems or multi-year contracts
• They need immediate ROI - they can't afford vanity tech
• They're agile enough to switch if something doesn't work
• Their spending reflects actual value, not inertia
So in summary - yes, startup spending signals what will succeed in the enterprise market.
📊The report also derived patterns about what categories are commanding startup dollars, how horizontal vs vertical AI apps are evolving, how roles and tooling shift, and how many of these startups began as consumer tools that moved enterprise-ward. Worth a read 👇
#AI #EnterpriseAdoption #Innovation
https://lnkd.in/gp-i4FMP

Dave Guevara
9mo
Report this post
Zain Kahn 's Superhuman did a nice summary of a very informing AI study by Andreessen Horowitz on Startup spends. Worth the read.
Venture Capital firm Andreessen Horowitz’s first AI Application Spending Report reveals where early-stage companies are putting their dollars. Using transaction data from Mercury, the report lists the top 50 AI tools startups can’t live without — and some surprise products made the list.
Experimentation is everywhere. Startups are testing multiple note-takers, coding assistants, and productivity apps at once, picking “their own flavor” of tools. New apps rise and fall at breakneck speed. “There’s a proliferation of tools,” says a16z partner Seema Amble. “It hasn’t just coalesced around one or two in each category.”
Copilots are leading the way. Despite the hype around autonomous AI agents, startups are still spending on AI tools that assist employees, helping them work faster and make smarter decisions rather than replacing them. Amble expects a gradual shift toward end-to-end AI agents as workflows mature.
The current frontrunners: OpenAI tops the list, followed by Anthropic. Vibe-coding tools like Replit and Cursor rank high while creative tools such as CapCut, Canva, Midjourney, Freepik, and ElevenLabs are also making waves — showing how quickly the line between consumer and enterprise in AI is blurring.
5 Comments

Keith King
8mo
Report this post
Show Me the Money in AI: Where the Real Value Lies in the AI Gold Rush
Beyond the Hype: Following the Innovation Rails
As the world races to build smarter AI models, billions are being poured into infrastructure—from chips to data centers—while questions mount over where the true economic value lies. According to venture capitalist Rohini Chakravarthy of NewBuild Venture Capital, the answer may not be in the models themselves, but in the “innovation rails”—the infrastructure and services that enable scalable, profitable AI deployment.
Key Insights
• The AI Value Chain Defined:
The AI ecosystem spans raw compute and data, model training, application development, and end-user delivery. Each layer transforms technical capability into business utility, but not all layers capture equal value.
• Infrastructure Is the Foundation:
Companies such as Nvidia, Oracle, and AWS dominate the hardware and cloud layer, providing the “rails” for AI deployment. Their execution capabilities and consumption-based models make them the early winners in monetization.
• Middle Layers Hold the Promise:
Between foundational models and end-user apps lies a lucrative middle ground—platforms that abstract complexity, offering APIs, orchestration tools, and plug-and-play AI functions for enterprises.
• Applications Face Pressure:
Many GenAI startups focused solely on chatbots or image tools are struggling with differentiation and cost efficiency. Their success increasingly depends on how well they leverage infrastructure partners and manage compute economics.
• Value Creation Shifts to Enablement:
As Chakravarthy notes, sustainable returns will come from “companies that make AI practical”—those that bridge innovation to execution, not just generate headlines.
Why It Matters
The next AI boom will favor builders of rails, not just riders. Investors and enterprises seeking lasting value must look beyond flashy applications to the infrastructure and integration layers powering them. In the evolving AI economy, profitability belongs to those who control the flow of intelligence—not just the output.
🔒 Ready or Not: CMMC Rule Cleared for DoD Contractors
👤 CEOs, COOs, CFOs, CISOs, CCOs, Heads of Procurement — the clock is ticking.
Proud to spotlight @Churchill & Harriman and Ken Peterson, CTPRP, for demonstrated leadership in CMMC readiness.
Why it matters
• You must be able to accurately attest to your CMMC environment.
• The Churchill & Harriman (C&H) delivery team has delivered or is currently working on over 50 CMMC Level 2 engagements.
• 30 years of successful execution across government and industry.
Next step
👉 Partner with validated experts to get C3PAO assessment-ready and protect what matters most.
🔗 https://lnkd.in/gp-WhRW4
📧 info@chus.com
@Ken Peterson
#CMMC #CyberSecurity #DefenseIndustrialBase #AuditReadiness #RiskManagement #DoD #Compliance

Rajeev Roy
9mo
Report this post
AI dollars reveal priorities, not hype.
a16z analyzed where 200k+ startups actually spend on AI. The data uncovers a clear hierarchy:
🔹 Foundational tools first: 60% of spend goes to horizontal apps (LLMs, meetings, creative tools). These are the new “utilities.”
🔹 From assistants to replacements: vertical apps split between augmenting humans and acting as “AI employees” in fields like engineering and law.
🔹 Code as culture shift: Replit ranking #3 signals AI-native coding is moving from side-projects to core enterprise workflows.
This is not speculation about the future: it is the current balance sheet of AI adoption.
If today’s spending sets the roadmap, which category will dominate in 3 years: utilities, AI coworkers, or AI-native platforms?
Full report: https://lnkd.in/g9J8ZSDm
#AI #Startups #VentureCapital #FutureOfWork

Kurella suman Datta
8mo
Report this post
Most AI startups promise transformation. Riff just raised $16M because they deliver it. The difference isn't technology—it's what happens after the demo.
Oslo-based Riff (formerly Databutton) just closed their Series A with Northzone leading the round. Total funding now sits at $21 million.
Here's what caught my attention:
• They solve the "vibe coding" problem plaguing AI development
• Platform connects real data with production-grade security
• Users get starter apps, templates, and expert guidance
• Results show higher satisfaction and better project completion
The gap they're filling is huge. Most AI tools generate impressive demos but fail in real business environments.
Riff's approach combines complexity, actual data, and security. This matters for industries like financial services, healthcare, and manufacturing where compliance isn't optional.
Their customer retention rates and project completion metrics tell the real story. When practitioners at all levels can build and deploy AI applications that create genuine business impact, everyone wins.
The platform democratizes AI development without sacrificing enterprise requirements. That's the sweet spot many companies have been searching for.
With this funding, they plan to empower thousands more professionals to build meaningful AI solutions.
What's your experience with AI development platforms? Are you seeing more success with business-ready solutions or still struggling with the demo-to-production gap?
#AI #TechFunding #AIDevelopment
𝗦𝗼𝘂𝗿𝗰𝗲꞉ https://lnkd.in/gRFGRXqP

To view or add a comment, sign in
Daniel Kempe
9mo
Report this post
Just saw Forbes dropped their 2025 AI 50 list and man, the landscape has shifted.
No longer about who can build the biggest model - now it's about who's actually making something useful with AI.
Noticing apps and practical tools taking center stage while OpenAI and Anthropic continue burning through cash ($81B combined - that's not a typo).
Really interesting to see infrastructure plays like Crusoe and Lambda rising to meet the compute demand. This is where the unsexy but critical innovation happens.
For founders building right now: the opportunity isn't necessarily in creating another foundational model. It's in solving real problems on top of what exists. The companies winning are the ones automating actual work in healthcare, legal, engineering, sales.
The AI gold rush continues, but the successful miners are selling pickaxes and finding specific veins worth digging, not just throwing money at bigger shovels.
What specific industry problems do you think are most ripe for AI solutions right now? https://lnkd.in/eEPmM8-U

## clean_text

Why AI adoption is blocked by traditional workplaces
This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Christopher Manning
1y
Report this post
The biggest blocker for the adoption of AI currently isn't technical AI developments but rather how to re-engineer traditional workplaces and workflows. That last mile can be hard.
Shaun Johnson
1y
Today we announce that AIX Ventures has led the Series A of Workhelix .
Every enterprise must have an AI transformation war room.
Deploying AI will lead to pricing power. When productivity improves through AI, gross- and operating- margins go up. This means price can decrease, bringing more demand and putting the competition on notice.
This isn't an optional transformation. It’s a race. Every enterprise AI transformation war room needs to be staffed with a team that understands the intricacies of the particular enterprise, the data behind the enterprise, economics, and the ability to lead the enterprise through the transformation. And the team needs to have an AI transformation software suite custom to their enterprise, to maximize transformation velocity.
Enter James Milin , Daniel Rock , Andrew McAfee , Erik Brynjolfsson , and Workhelix team. The Workhelix team deploys engineers trained on the Workhelix task-based approach to understand the tasks being completed, specific to a particular enterprise. The engineers partner with the enterprise’s leaders to assess these tasks, and to map bundles of tasks to GenAI solutions.
The war room team members can then partner with various organizations within the enterprise to implement, monitor solution efficacy, and reassess. An effective war room should have every organization engaged in this process, iterating to the best version of that organization within the context of the bleeding edge Gen AI solutions today.
Today AIX Ventures is leading the Workhelix $15 million Series A, along with Andrew Ng 's AI Fund , Accenture Technology Ventures BV, Reid Hoffman , Jeff Dean , Yann LeCun , Sebastian Thrun , Paul Daugherty , Mira Murati, and Jeff Wilke.
I spoke with Rebecca Szkutak at TechCrunch about the company and its massive opportunity to transform our economy and workforce: https://lnkd.in/gJ2pufUc
Enterprise leaders need to have one transformation metric top-of-mind: what % of our enterprise tasks are completed by machines?

💰 Startups just revealed what they're spending a massive amount of VC money on... Yup, it's AI.
🔎 Andreessen Horowitz analyzed 200,000 startups’ actual AI spend. Not surveys, not hype. CA$H MONEY 💸
💥 60% of AI spend goes to horizontal apps like OpenAI , Anthropic , Canva , Notion , Freepik . And vertical apps still command 40%, with many shifting from augmentation to full workflow automation.
And the signal is LOUD:
🔊 Creative AI tools (image, video, avatars, voice) are among the top 5 spend categories.
🔊 Replit ranked #3 overall, proof that “vibe coding” is no longer a meme. AI is literally building apps that ship.
🔊 In verticals, 12 of 17 top vertical AI apps are augmenting humans, while 5 are already functioning as full “AI employees” (e.g., AI IT desks, AI legal tech, GTM agents, etc.).
💡 Translation for VCs:
➡️ You're not always funding headcount growth; Founders are channeling huge chunks of capital into AI apps.
➡️ Infrastructure raises steal the headlines... but real buy-in is unfolding at the application layer.
➡️ This isn’t experimentation. Startup budgets already treat AI tools as essential, not optional.
➡️ This isn’t an adoption curve. It’s adoption baked into burn rates.
➡️ The next AI unicorns won’t necessarily be infra monsters; they’ll be the apps quietly swallowing VC dollars, one Amex swipe at a time.
👉 Full report: https://lnkd.in/dMEZ-rdE

Report this post
🚀 Where Startups are spending their dollars💵
Not just another report on AI tools. This is interesting - the guys at a16z just released a AI Spending Report that looks at where 200,000+ startups are actually spending their money on AI tools.
💡Why this matters?
We know that startups are early indicators of adoption trends because they experiment faster so the pattern can be like this 👉 Start-ups adopt → prove ROI → enterprises follow couple of years later.
🚀 Startups are ideal testers because:
• They're not locked into legacy systems or multi-year contracts
• They need immediate ROI - they can't afford vanity tech
• They're agile enough to switch if something doesn't work
• Their spending reflects actual value, not inertia
So in summary - yes, startup spending signals what will succeed in the enterprise market.
📊The report also derived patterns about what categories are commanding startup dollars, how horizontal vs vertical AI apps are evolving, how roles and tooling shift, and how many of these startups began as consumer tools that moved enterprise-ward. Worth a read 👇
#AI #EnterpriseAdoption #Innovation

Dave Guevara
9mo
Report this post
Zain Kahn 's Superhuman did a nice summary of a very informing AI study by Andreessen Horowitz on Startup spends. Worth the read.
Venture Capital firm Andreessen Horowitz’s first AI Application Spending Report reveals where early-stage companies are putting their dollars. Using transaction data from Mercury, the report lists the top 50 AI tools startups can’t live without — and some surprise products made the list.
Experimentation is everywhere. Startups are testing multiple note-takers, coding assistants, and productivity apps at once, picking “their own flavor” of tools. New apps rise and fall at breakneck speed. “There’s a proliferation of tools,” says a16z partner Seema Amble. “It hasn’t just coalesced around one or two in each category.”
Copilots are leading the way. Despite the hype around autonomous AI agents, startups are still spending on AI tools that assist employees, helping them work faster and make smarter decisions rather than replacing them. Amble expects a gradual shift toward end-to-end AI agents as workflows mature.
The current frontrunners: OpenAI tops the list, followed by Anthropic. Vibe-coding tools like Replit and Cursor rank high while creative tools such as CapCut, Canva, Midjourney, Freepik, and ElevenLabs are also making waves — showing how quickly the line between consumer and enterprise in AI is blurring.

Show Me the Money in AI: Where the Real Value Lies in the AI Gold Rush
Beyond the Hype: Following the Innovation Rails
As the world races to build smarter AI models, billions are being poured into infrastructure—from chips to data centers—while questions mount over where the true economic value lies. According to venture capitalist Rohini Chakravarthy of NewBuild Venture Capital, the answer may not be in the models themselves, but in the “innovation rails”—the infrastructure and services that enable scalable, profitable AI deployment.
Key Insights
• The AI Value Chain Defined:
The AI ecosystem spans raw compute and data, model training, application development, and end-user delivery. Each layer transforms technical capability into business utility, but not all layers capture equal value.
• Infrastructure Is the Foundation:
Companies such as Nvidia, Oracle, and AWS dominate the hardware and cloud layer, providing the “rails” for AI deployment. Their execution capabilities and consumption-based models make them the early winners in monetization.
• Middle Layers Hold the Promise:
Between foundational models and end-user apps lies a lucrative middle ground—platforms that abstract complexity, offering APIs, orchestration tools, and plug-and-play AI functions for enterprises.
• Applications Face Pressure:
Many GenAI startups focused solely on chatbots or image tools are struggling with differentiation and cost efficiency. Their success increasingly depends on how well they leverage infrastructure partners and manage compute economics.
• Value Creation Shifts to Enablement:
As Chakravarthy notes, sustainable returns will come from “companies that make AI practical”—those that bridge innovation to execution, not just generate headlines.
Why It Matters
The next AI boom will favor builders of rails, not just riders. Investors and enterprises seeking lasting value must look beyond flashy applications to the infrastructure and integration layers powering them. In the evolving AI economy, profitability belongs to those who control the flow of intelligence—not just the output.

Rajeev Roy

AI dollars reveal priorities, not hype.
a16z analyzed where 200k+ startups actually spend on AI. The data uncovers a clear hierarchy:
🔹 Foundational tools first: 60% of spend goes to horizontal apps (LLMs, meetings, creative tools). These are the new “utilities.”
🔹 From assistants to replacements: vertical apps split between augmenting humans and acting as “AI employees” in fields like engineering and law.
🔹 Code as culture shift: Replit ranking #3 signals AI-native coding is moving from side-projects to core enterprise workflows.
This is not speculation about the future: it is the current balance sheet of AI adoption.
If today’s spending sets the roadmap, which category will dominate in 3 years: utilities, AI coworkers, or AI-native platforms?
Full report: https://lnkd.in/g9J8ZSDm
#AI #Startups #VentureCapital #FutureOfWork

Most AI startups promise transformation. Riff just raised $16M because they deliver it. The difference isn't technology—it's what happens after the demo.
Oslo-based Riff (formerly Databutton) just closed their Series A with Northzone leading the round. Total funding now sits at $21 million.
Here's what caught my attention:
• They solve the "vibe coding" problem plaguing AI development
• Platform connects real data with production-grade security
• Users get starter apps, templates, and expert guidance
• Results show higher satisfaction and better project completion
The gap they're filling is huge. Most AI tools generate impressive demos but fail in real business environments.
Riff's approach combines complexity, actual data, and security. This matters for industries like financial services, healthcare, and manufacturing where compliance isn't optional.
Their customer retention rates and project completion metrics tell the real story. When practitioners at all levels can build and deploy AI applications that create genuine business impact, everyone wins.
The platform democratizes AI development without sacrificing enterprise requirements. That's the sweet spot many companies have been searching for.
With this funding, they plan to empower thousands more professionals to build meaningful AI solutions.
What's your experience with AI development platforms? Are you seeing more success with business-ready solutions or still struggling with the demo-to-production gap?
#AI #TechFunding #AIDevelopment

Daniel Kempe
9mo
Report this post
Just saw Forbes dropped their 2025 AI 50 list and man, the landscape has shifted.
No longer about who can build the biggest model - now it's about who's actually making something useful with AI.
Noticing apps and practical tools taking center stage while OpenAI and Anthropic continue burning through cash ($81B combined - that's not a typo).
Really interesting to see infrastructure plays like Crusoe and Lambda rising to meet the compute demand. This is where the unsexy but critical innovation happens.
For founders building right now: the opportunity isn't necessarily in creating another foundational model. It's in solving real problems on top of what exists. The companies winning are the ones automating actual work in healthcare, legal, engineering, sales.
The AI gold rush continues, but the successful miners are selling pickaxes and finding specific veins worth digging, not just throwing money at bigger shovels.
What specific industry problems do you think are most ripe for AI solutions right now? https://lnkd.in/eEPmM8-U

• Results show higher satisfaction and better project completion
The gap they're filling is huge. Most AI tools generate impressive demos but fail in real business environments.
Riff's approach combines complexity, actual data, and security. This matters for industries like financial services, healthcare, and manufacturing where compliance isn't optional.
Their customer retention rates and project completion metrics tell the real story. When practitioners at all levels can build and deploy AI applications that create genuine business impact, everyone wins.
The platform democratizes AI development without sacrificing enterprise requirements. That's the sweet spot many companies have been searching for.
With this funding, they plan to empower thousands more professionals to build meaningful AI solutions.
What's your experience with AI development platforms? Are you seeing more success with business-ready solutions or still struggling with the demo-to-production gap?
